data.title: Realty Income Is Still Looking For New Ways To Grow (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Today, we circle back to net lease real estate, the bread and butter of REITer's Digest. Over the past several months, I've published a variety of articles discussing net lease real estate from various perspectives. Recently, I published an article on the Vanguard Real Estate ETF (VNQ), where I discussed the impacts of stabilizing net lease capitalization rates. More recently, I published an article on Agree Realty (ADC), outlining the company's high-quality portfolio and emphasis on creditworthy tenants. With an uncertain macroeconomic backdrop, a high quality portfolio appears to be earning its stripes.

Just last month, I published an article covering Realty Income (O), the blue chip of net lease real estate. One of the oldest publicly traded REITs, and the largest in net lease by a mile, the big O is a behemoth in net lease real estate.

I mentioned this in nearly every particle that I publish on O, the company owns more properties than many of its competitors combined. O recently announced another incremental increase to the company's monthly dividend finally reaching the mark of $0.27 per share per month. With another dividend increase in the bank, let's dive back into O and review prior coverage.

Review of Prior Coverage

Last month, I published an article covering O's Q3 earnings release. The article was centered around finding value in the net lease sector. Beyond analyzing the high points of the company's Q3 earnings report, we dove into the company's dividend yield spread against the 10 year treasury.

Following a period of prolonged compression, I noted that O's yield spread against the risk free rate had begun to expand since the beginning of November.

As it stood more than one month ago, the company's dividend yield was finally paying more than 100 basis points more than the 10 year treasury. Today, the company continues to be priced at a similar premium.As the behemoth company continued to chart its growth trajectory billions of dollars of real estate every three months, I continue to emphasize the significant change going on at the monthly dividend company.

A key point across my prior coverage of the company has been O's change from a net lease landlord that bought Taco Bell restaurants and childcare facilities to the biggest on the block. Over time, the company's business has changed almost completely. Today, the company is hardly the net lease REIT that we knew over the past 50 years. Nowadays, the company owns a gigantic international portfolio that also includes data centers, casinos, and cold storage facilities.

For more on that, I suggest checking out my article, O, How You've Changed.

What's New At O?

Since the publication of my prior article discussing the rapid changes being made to O's business model, the company has continued to make changes to their platform. Over the past year, there have been several new changes to the company, many of which are significant. Let's dive in and get specific.

Over the past year, there's been a relatively quiet hubbub around O regarding their venture into the private spheres. Note that 'spheres' is in fact plural there. Across coverage of O and in the comments, I see misconceptions around their ventures into both private capital and private credit.

The important distinction is that their new private capital business includes a new perpetual life fund that will invest in net lease assets for a management fee. Meanwhile, private credit means that O has actually begun lending to tenants rather than just leasing the real estate as their traditional business entails.

Neither of these are straight down O's historical fairway.

CEO Sumit Roy commented on adding a perpetual life fund to O's busy business.

As we establish ourselves in the private capital arena, our long track record of producing equity-like total returns with bond-like stability is resonating with investors. We recently launched a perpetual life fund and expect that initiative will provide additional capital to support our growth objectives and enhance our liquidity position.

Managing private capital means that the company is now responsible for separating assets into either the O bucket or the O's clients bucket, creating a difficult decision that must be made by the company's management team. In the past, we've seen other businesses try similar moves to drive scalable revenue from a platform when their cost of capital is no longer permissive. There's some overlapping similarity W.P. Carey's (WPC) failed venture into the TPAM space.

Going one step further, it seems even stranger that O would begin making loans to their tenants beyond signing long term leases. CEO Sumit Roy described that making a loan to a tenant is not so different from a sale leaseback of 10 or 20 years.

Bennett RoseCitigroup Inc., Research Division

I wanted to ask you a little bit about the loans you made in Europe in the quarter. It looks like you found some very healthy opportunities on the yield side. Can you share maybe who those were with and if you would expect to see significant opportunity in that kind of arena over the next quarters or so?

Sumit RoyPresident, CEO & Director

So Smedes, as we've said, when we started going down the credit investment path of one of the verticals that we wanted to sort of lean into, there was a strategic rationale behind it. We were -- what we had shared with you was we were already long the clients. We were doing 15-year, 20-year sale leasebacks with these clients. And we were long the credit. We were very comfortable the credit.

In both cases, you're locking up the capital as a commitment based on the clients' financials. However, it seems strange that O, a real estate company, has decided to foray into banking ventures where they're making capital contributions directly to the balance sheet of a tenant. Usually, a company like O would want to leverage their real estate expertise to use their capital to buy real estate, rather than provide private loans to existing tenants. Perhaps in a downside, O could loan a tenant money to pay the rent they are owed?

This type of investment also aligns with their recent deal with Blackstone (BX) to purchase an $800 million slice of preferred equity from City Center in Las Vegas.

This is an interesting deal that roughly aligns with O's past preferred equity deals with BX. In this case, the company has made an $800 million perpetual loan backed by the real estate at City Center. Details of the deal can be found below including transaction metrics.

The perpetual preferred equity investment is expected to carry an initial unlevered rate of return to Realty Income (O) of 7.4% with annual, capped escalators starting on the fifth anniversary of the closing. What's more, the investment is subject to an early redemption premium payable to Realty Income...

From my perspective, this points towards an interesting trend for O. Over the years, I've talked about the company encountering challenges around their supersized growth over the past decade. By acquiring more than 10,000 properties as well as competing REITs, O has found themselves in a precarious position of needing to deploy enormous amounts of capital in a scalable format. Nontraditional deals like lending to tenants or buying an $800 million preferred slice out of City Center are opportunities for a company like O to deploy capital as fast as they need to continue to grow the business.

However, from the perspective of a shareholder, many of these deals appear different from O's historical path. With that in mind, each of these investments is absorbed into the existing shares that are held by O's investors. I look at this as an overall dilution of the quality of my investment where a portfolio of high quality net lease assets has now been turned into an internationally diversified portfolio of general investments.

Conclusion

O is trying to grow. Looking at the company's lofty guidance, the company needs to deploy billions of dollars of capital every quarter to meet investor expectations. From my personal experience in the net lease sector, it is difficult to acquire that volume of assets without allowing your quality parameters to begin to slip. In the case of O, the company has moved towards a model of deploying capital at scale, rather than looking for incremental investment opportunities like ADC.

I see this as a significant long-term risk to the health of O's business. They venture further and further from their traditional business means investors' shares are backed by a constantly evolving portfolio of assets. I would strongly prefer disciplined growth backed by prudent capital recycling. With that, O earns a Hold rating.

data.title: McCormick: A Flavor Utility Paying A Policy Surcharge
data.description:
McCormick’s flavor franchise remains healthy and in a phased restart in volume-driven growth even as it faces tariffs and an expansion in Mexico. On current stock prices, this company appears to be a high-quality dividend-growing compounder temporarily stymied by policy, rather than a broken franchise.

Business Quality: Flavor as Everyday Infrastructure

McCormick & Company (MKC) has evolved from a local spice vendor, established in 1889, to the current worldwide leader in herbs, spices, seasoning blends, and sauces, with a product presence in around 150 different countries. The company operates with a twofold business model that in essence has two closely integrated engines. The Consumer segment, which contributes a bit over half of total sales, markets branded spices, seasoning mixes, hot sauces, mustards, and condiments to consumer markets, including grocery, mass, and e-commerce, through its brands like McCormick, Old Bay, Lawry’s, Schwartz, Ducros, Frank’s RedHot, French’s, and Cholula.

The Flavor Solutions business, which approaches forty percent of sales, is upstream. This business develops customized seasoning solutions, marinades, coatings, and flavorings for packaged food companies and restaurant chains, where this seasoning solution becomes part of their own recipe. Replacing this solution in their system involves not merely a pricing component but also flavor, supply chain considerations, labeling, and in some cases, regulatory reform. There are costs of switching that a commodity company does not incur.

What sets McCormick apart structurally is that it has control over the flavor stack from soup to nuts. McCormick sources whole world supplies of herbs and spices, has a robust sensory and R&D platform, and has assets like their long-running Flavor Forecast initiative that taps into early trends in flavor. This data for both their consumer side of their business and their B2B side, which creates a closed loop where data from consumers and chef influences becomes new product formulations for foodservice and consumer product companies, which then creates volume in their institutions that then creates economies of scale in their sourcing and manufacturing that drives their shelf business. This has a low-visibility role in building new innovations, where a company like a snack company that needs a new product in chili-lime powder, a quick-service chain that wants a different fried chicken seasoning, where McCormick has all their own knowledge of the application of those types of goods.

Hot sauce and condiments are a perfect example of this moat. The purchase of Frank’s RedHot and French’s in 2017, followed by Cholula in 2020, pushed McCormick even further into spaces where brand loyalty is very high and usage per serving is infinitesimal. Trading down from a favorite hot sauce brand or a favorite mayonnaise brand merely to cut costs costs precious little in a grocery basket but translates to a sharp degradation of quality; as such, sensitivity to pricing has low elasticity. McCormick’s plan to consolidate McCormick de México, where their McCormick Mayonesa brand is a leading brand in that category, further integrates this multi-channel, multi-brand heat and condiment offering.

Positioning for Uncertainty

The environment for packaged food companies has moved from straight-cost inflation to a complicated balance of consumer normalizing and trade policy. McCormick needed to demonstrate that their model is robust for both. Following intense pricing in 2022-2023, FY 2024 was a repair year, with net sales increasing around 1% to around $6.7 billion, with organic sales also increasing 1%, led by modest pricing and a small positive volume/mix contribution. Consumer sales increased around 1% to around $3.9 billion, with Flavor Solutions sales also increasing around 1% to around $2.9 billion, despite being impacted by a small divestiture in their canning business.

Gross profit margin improvement of around 90 basis points to around 38 1/2% of sales reflected product mix, past-year pricing initiatives, as well as benefits from Comprehensive Continuous Improvement and Global Operating Effectiveness initiatives. Operating income did grow to a bit over $1.0 billion, with earnings per share rising from around $2.52 to a bit over $2.92. On an adjusted basis, EPS of around $2.95 marked a near 9% improvement from a year ago. This, of course, does not count as hyper-growth, but it indicates that the engine is beginning to repair itself from that inflation shock.

Management’s approach for the rest of 2025 has been to re-emphasize volume. So, their goal has been differentiated volume-led growth, not a series of further broader-based price rises, and this approach has now shown up in the trend, as McCormick has reported several quarters of volume-led organic sales growth. In third-quarter 2025, net sales were around 3% higher, with total sales of about $1.7 billion, organic sales of about 2%, with volume and mix driving over a point of that. This is a very positive indicator that, for a product in a mature category, their brands and innovation are doing their job.

This resilience is rooted in their categories. When inflation affects households, consumers will want to trade down their protein sources and starches, but they will still want spices and hot sauce, which are relatively cheap ways of upgrading less expensive core ingredients. This so-called small luxury attribute provides a resilience benefit to McCormick’s portfolios that many center store categories lack.

There is, however, a new stress in tariffs. A general import tariff in the U.S. and a special surcharge for goods imported from China are considerations for a company like McCormick, which sources crops such as pepper, cinnamon, and vanilla from their origins. While gross tariffs are now seen as tens of millions of dollars in this year, with total gross annualized tariffs about twice that amount. For a company with over $1 billion in operating income, this represents a significant policy tax. McCormick is, in fact, not passing this total amount on in total in terms of pricing. The company takes this amount in exchange for protecting volume trends, as well as their brands. On the other hand, the company is not merely playing defense. Indeed, the agreement to acquire an additional 25% stake in McCormick de México from long-standing partner Grupo Herdez, in which it would take ownership to 75%, seems a shrewd move to further capitalize on a structurally growing market and secure a position in condiments and sauces. The JV provides around $800 million in net sales, with sales in mid-single digits, with a strong franchise in mayonnaise and other condiments. Post-acquisition, it would increase condiments and sauces to around one-fifth of McCormick’s worldwide sales, and then emerge as a sales, margin, and adjusted EPS accretive transaction in the first full year of acquisition. This is classic McCormick, where it seeks to capitalize on a world of access where their fundamental flavor platform would work and where private label would be structurally challenged.

Financials: What the Numbers Reveal

Compared to the 2024 base and 2025 run rate, McCormick appears to be a consistent but not exciting compounder with earnings being temporarily impacted by tariffs. Net sales for 2024 did increase slightly to $6.7 billion, with organic sales increasing 1%. Revenue for the Consumer Segment was $3.9 billion, with Flavor Solutions selling $2.9 billion, also increasing 1% in both cases. Gross margin did increase around a percentage point due to a favorable mix, pricing, and cost savings, with adjusted operating income growing in the low digits to over $1.0 billion.

Operating cash flow was just below $1.0 billion, with capex in the mid-$200 millions, leaving free cash flow in the mid-$600 millions, or high single-digit multiples of sales. The company's balance sheet finished 2024 with net leverage in low- to mid-3x EBITDA, which, while rich for a staple, was not egregious in a company with this amount of stability in cash flow and this long history of access to credit markets.

The cadence over the period through 2025 has now reinforced both the positive trends and the challenges. In 1Q 2025, net sales were approximating $1.6 billion, effectively unchanged from the previous year as 2% volume and mix were mitigated by adverse pricing. Adjusted EPS was just above $0.60, a slight decrement from the previous year and below market expectations, as revenue investments and early tariffs dampened margins. In the second quarter, net sales were up to $1.66 billion, with organic sales of 2% and adjusted EPS comparatively flat from a year ago but ahead of expectations, owing to working in efficiency. The third quarter then marked a volume-driven period, with net sales of 3% increase, organic sales of 2%, and adjusted EPS of around $0.85, which was slightly above market expectations, despite a sharp gross margin decrement of over a full percentage point due to tariffs as well as commodity-driven pressures.

The aggregate impact from this outlook perspective is reflected in guidance. Following the 2024 print, management has guided 2025 adjusted EPS in a range of a little above $3.00 per share for low single-digit net sales growth. Following the third-quarter 2025 earnings and updated estimates of tariffs, this EPS range has been reduced by a small amount, but sales continue to be flat to low single-digit growth in reported sales and low single digits in constant currency. This means that the fundamental driver of demand and mix is about where management thought it would be, but a policy surcharge and a few commodities are costing a few cents per share.

Valuation: What Are You Paying for Quality?

At a recent share price in the mid-$60s, McCormick now trades around 22 times trailing results, 20 times current-year adjusted EPS guidance, with a multiple in the mid-teens for EV/EBITDA, and just above three times for EV/Sales. This, of course, remains a premium compared with mainstream packaged food companies like General Mills and Kraft Heinz, which can maintain high-single-digit to low-teens multiples for P/E ratios, as well as low-digit multiples for EV/EBITDA ratios, but represents a sharp discount from McCormick’s former valuation, where it has traded in a range around a high 20s multiple for P/E ratios and a high teens ratio for EV/EBITDA.

From a cash flow perspective, free cash flow in the mid-$600 millions seems to warrant a high single-digit FCF yield on EV and a mid-single-digit yield on equity. With low single-digit sales growth, additional gross-margin expansion in the low single digits as a rough estimate of further improvement, excluding the hit from tariffs, and a return of capex to a normal run rate of a quarter billion per year, a free cash flow in the $700-800 range over the next few years seems surprisingly conservative. On a simplistic discounted cash flow model with 2-3% organic growth in these cash flows, using a mid-single-digit Cyr, a reasonable equity value would range from around low $70s. This would imply a wide range of percentage gains above current market prices, as well as a dividend yield in the high 2% range.

Rather, it is a question of whether a multiple of 20 for a flavor utility with a storied dividend growth story, limited cyclicality, and a temporary tariff overhang, compared with other places where one can deploy funds with a shot at low double-digit returns, let alone mid-single-digit returns, is all that compelling. To my mind, it’s a discount for policy risk, but not a discount due to a lack of faith in the business.

Insiders and Sentiment

The ownership structure data also reveal that institutions own a vast majority of shares in McCormick, followed by a relatively small percentage but substantial in value of ownership by insiders and legacy family members. This means that fluctuations of this stock are closely correlated with institutional risk appetite, where factor shifts out of the likes of staples can be damaging regardless of performance, but there are also a lot of long-only investors who favor stability and dividend enhancement. There has been relatively limited insider trading, which has been mostly one-sided in terms of sales, though this has been of a planned nature among management rather than a result of large-scale opportunistic sales. There has not been a noticeable trend of heavy insider buying, but also limited derivative interest, which suggests that, while there are clearly concerns over valuation, McCormick is by no means a battlefield short. The key message from this data, however, would be found in the multiple. McCormick has seen a sharp compression of its P/E ratio over the past two years despite a rise in EPS, which means that rather than a sharp fall in earnings, most of the fall in share price has been de-rating. This “quality at any price blues would set up a relatively balanced risk-reward profile where news becomes a movable force without needing to cover themselves in glory.

Catalysts On the Horizon

The most explicit catalyst would be having greater clarity and, of course, a mitigation of tariffs. McCormick currently has a substantial gross tariff exposure on an annualized basis, with a major part of this expected to hit in the current year. Having a positive change in trade policy, through exceptions, bilateral agreements, or a shift in policy attitudes, would immediately benefit gross margin. Even without a positive trade policy shift, reformulation, supply chain shifts, and ongoing expense management should help mitigate gross margin over a multi-year period.

The second key catalyst would be the consummation and integration of McCormick de México. Upon consolidation of this acquisition, investors would then realize the full benefit of a mid-single-digit growth condiments and sauces business, as opposed to equity earnings from a consolidation adjustment. Since this acquisition would be on par with other branded food companies and accretive to margins and EPS in the first-year post-acquisition, a clean integration and accretion would help to change market perceptions from slow U.S. spice company to global condiments platform.

In addition to these two major levers, a host of other, albeit cumulative and smaller, drivers are also in play. McCormick’s R&D pipeline for innovation and renovation remains robust, with new seasoning varieties, gourmet line refreshes, and packaging enhancements all working to maintain shelf. Its heat platform also retains further extension in new formats and related ventures. And in general, of course, the fundamental transformation due to increased home preparation, particularly among younger consumers attempting to cut back on restaurant expenses, remains supportive of categories in which McCormick remains over-indexed. Should interest rates move further downward from current levels, McCormick’s long streak of consecutive dividend growth and mid-single-digit EPS expansion may also find it back in a dividend-growth and quality income screen.

Risks: Where Could This Break?

The key risk with the current structure is that tariffs end up being both higher and more enduring than current management and market expectations. McCormick cannot near-source most of their key agricultural ingredients; it will continue to import spices from a variety of different countries. Should current import tariffs in the U.S. and surcharges on Chinese imports continue for a long period of time, maybe even broaden, it would eventually run out of easy ways to mitigate this problem and either settle for lower margins or attempt to drive even sharper price hikes. With a softer macro setting, this could hurt share in more price-sensitive product lines.

A second is the nature of competition and retail dynamics in the spice category and overall. Sustained pressure on consumer budgets could speed a trade-down into private labels and smaller, regional players. With their own profit margins being compressed, retailers may conclude it wise to devote even more shelf space and promotional support to their proprietary labels. A misreading of this dynamic balance of pricing, promotion, and package re-positionings, of course, poses a certain risk to McCormick even if their upscale brands continue to flourish.

In the Mexico deal, risks of classical execution and capital allocation exist. With a significant upfront expenditure for a further minority stake, not only has a continuous momentum in Mexican condiments and sauces been presumed, but also a seamless integration with a trusted partnership over the years. Should consumer demand slow down, increased competition, regulatory, or tax shocks appear, then the accretion of value would be deferred when leverage is increased and flexibility limited.

There are also traditional risks related to the food industry, such as product recalls, contamination, and regulatory issues related to ingredients and packaging. McCormick has a history of quality and is now reformulating to meet regulatory requirements, but a major outbreak can impair brand reputation and divert management attention when Mexico and tariffs are still being digested. Lastly, even with de-rating, McCormick remains richly valued in relation to true staples. A further year of EPS guidance reductions or a surprise related to tariffs would further compress valuations, leaving shareholders with lackluster performance despite improvement.

Conclusion: Still Worth the Price?

McCormick, to my mind, remains a “flavor utility” – a globally scaled, dual-engine business with deep, sustained compounding advantages and a frugal capital allocation mindset. The current issues related to tariffs and a slight degradation in profits appears to be a cyclical, political hurdle, whereas volume trends, improvement of margins, and expansion in Mexico are pointing to sustained compounding of this business.

With a forward ROI of around 20 and a TEV/Ebitda ratio in the mid-teens, a dividend yield of nearly 3%, and a streak of nearly forty years of dividend hikes, McCormick appears to be a superior holding for those with deep pockets, who can digest policy swings. Though it has limited legs, with EPS traffic, EPS expansion, a stable dividend, and possibly some re-rating as tariff uncertainties are normalized, I think this specialty compounder has a benign risk-reward profile. On this analysis and remaining consistent with the notion that McCormick is paying a policy surcharge for a temporary period on a solid flavor platform, my recommendation for MKC remains a Buy.

data.title: Giving Thanks For Market Strength
data.description:
As 2025 nears its end and holiday season continues, investors may look to take some gains and rebalance portfolios to long-term targets

In Short

  • S&P 500 eked out a seventh straight monthly gain (+0.2% in November; +17.8% year-to-date), with breadth improving as the equal-weighted index outperformed.
  • Early signs of strong holiday shopping were muddied by underlying consumer trends and weaker sentiment, but we remain optimistic in the medium term.
  • For remainder of the year, the focus will likely be on the December Federal Reserve meeting; consider year-end rebalancing following a strong year for asset classes.

The Month in Markets

Investors may give thanks for the month-end rally that allowed the S&P 500 to narrowly capture its seventh-straight month of gains, up 0.2% in November and up 17.8% year-to-date. For reference, the index was down 5% at one point during the month, which marked the end of what was an exceptionally resilient equity rally with only a few shallow pullbacks (of less than 3%) since April.

The negative price action in early November reflected a more “risk-off” mood and can be linked to general economic uncertainty, in part related to a lack of U.S. government data, more-hawkish-than-expected Fed sentiment during the prior meeting, and some cooling of artificial intelligence-related optimism. This caused market leadership to shift from growth- and momentum-driven assets (which have driven market returns for the last few years) into more defensive pockets of the market, boosting the quality factor over momentum to its highest relative level since the tariff-induced shocks in March and early April. For stocks, this meant strength in sectors such as health care, but at the broader asset class level, fixed income and commodities held up well in November.

U.S. Quality vs. Momentum Factors Relative Performance – Monthly Spread & 12M Rolling Spread

The losses were then erased by the end of the month, apart from the technology-focused Nasdaq Composite, which ended with a loss of 1.4%, though down as much as 7% at one point during the month. The reversal in sentiment was largely tied to renewed hopes for a Federal Reserve rate cut in December, with a now roughly 80% chance of a quarter-point cut; in the depths of the pullback, the odds were below 25%. The S&P 500 rallied 3.7% during Thanksgiving week, marking its best performance during the shortened trading week since 2008, driven primarily by the Magnificent 71 stocks, apart from Nvidia, which was the biggest detractor from the index. Notably, though the top stocks in the index rallied, the equal-weighted index slightly outperformed the market-weighted index, showing some broadened strength within the S&P 500. In fact, November was the first month of the year in which the 10 largest stocks in the index detracted from total returns, while the bottom 490 propelled it into positive territory.

S&P 500 Monthly Returns Decomposition – Top 10 Largest Stock vs. Bottom 490

Still, there is little doubt that the Fed is in a tricky position coming into its December 10 meeting. The belatedly released September U.S. nonfarm payrolls report, with cumulative revisions of -33,000, coupled with an upward tick in jobless claims, has signaled that hiring is slowing, and the Fed is loath to sit idle and watch low hiring become fast firing. It is important to note that this is the last official jobs data the Fed will receive before the meeting, as a combined October and November payrolls report is slated to drop on December 16.

Tale of Two Consumers

Meanwhile, inflation appears to be on a downward trajectory, particularly given recent downward pressure in housing prices. However, the picture is less clear with the Fed’s preferred metric, Personal Consumption Expenditure (PCE), delayed due to the government shutdown. Though September’s Consumer Price Index (CPI) proved lighter than expected, investors won’t get a glimpse of fresher figures until later in December. Inflation concerns are the other part of the Fed’s dual mandate, and it is part of the reason why the “K-shaped economy” has been popularly referenced as of late.

Simply put, the upper part of the “K” refers to higher-income households seeing incomes and wealth rise, while the lower part points to lower-income households struggling with weaker income gains. We’ve seen that inflation is still a meaningful concern, particularly for lower-income households. For example, in November, President Trump announced a decision to roll back tariffs on coffee, beef, bananas and orange juice to alleviate upward pressure on food prices. However, as Fed Chair Jerome Powell mentioned in the July Fed rate decision press conference, the impact of tariff action on inflation could take some time to translate.

We can look to businesses for insight into how the U.S. consumer is faring. The third-quarter earnings season showed continued strength, with S&P 500 reporting blended earnings growth of 13.4% as of November (over 97% of companies have reported). If this is the actual growth rate for the quarter, it will mark the fourth consecutive quarter of double-digit earnings growth for the index. Two big-box retailers illustrating some divergence in consumer behavior were Walmart and Target. Walmart, known as a discounted retailer, raised its sales outlook for the year and commented on an influx in business from higher-income households. Meanwhile, Target lowered its sales outlook and cited softness in demand on some discretionary items.

Consumer sentiment has stumbled recently. The University of Michigan’s consumer sentiment index came in below forecasts to a near-record low of 50.3, down 30% from a year ago. November consumer confidence dropped to 88.7, its lowest level since April. Meanwhile, early data for Black Friday showed consumers shopping at encouraging levels, although underlying spending patterns are painting a muddier picture. While these aren’t the only datapoints to consider, consumer spending accounts for about two-thirds of economic activity, and the holiday season is considered a barometer of how shoppers are faring in the current economic climate.

As 2025 nears its end and the holiday season continues, investors may look to take some gains and rebalance portfolios to long-term targets. The S&P 500 is on track for a third consecutive year of double-digit returns, a phenomenon that has only occurred six times since 1939. As we look ahead to 2026, we remain confident in the medium-term outlook, supported by several factors, including the next phase of monetary easing, fiscal stimulus, ongoing economic resilience and inflation, which has proven broadly better than feared.

In our view, it is especially important now that investors ensure portfolios are diversified across multiple asset classes, providing a balance of exposures across public and private markets that blend attractive sources of risk-adjusted returns. While artificial intelligence still has room to run as a tailwind for technology stocks, investors should use year-end as an opportunity to rebalance risks and ensure diversification.

Portfolio Implications

Equities saw some dispersion, with value outperforming growth, small and midcaps outpacing large caps, and developed non-U.S. outpacing U.S. stocks. We maintain an overweight to small and midcaps on the expectation that these companies will continue to benefit from rate cuts, deregulation and pro-business policies. Developed non-U.S. equities should continue to benefit from the rotation as well, although there are opportunities to be more tactical on signs of economic divergence. Otherwise, we maintain an “at-target” view across equities. Admittedly, short-term political volatility may create some pressure. However, any larger pullbacks (10% or more) could be an appropriate trigger to add risk, especially for those holding excess cash. In this more challenging environment, we favor employing active management to select companies with high earnings visibility.

Fixed income was higher and performed well during the month’s volatility. We remain constructive on investment-grade fixed income but see opportunities to deploy cash tactically, adding and/or shortening duration based on the movement of rates. While geopolitical and tariff risks persist, we maintain that the worst-case scenarios seem less likely and that outcomes should not significantly affect credit markets. Further expected cuts of U.S. policy rates by the Fed should continue to boost performance of U.S. fixed income, but the One Big Beautiful Bill Act has added some uncertainty, as investors may continue to demand higher term premiums to compensate for the risk of holding long-term government bonds at a time when large federal deficits are creating uncertainty about future economic stability. Multi-sector bond funds may be an appropriate vehicle to consider, given the levers a manager can opportunistically pull across sector, duration profile and region.

With a fading liquidity drought and a recent pickup in mergers and acquisitions and other deal activity, we believe significant opportunities still exist within Private Markets, as investors should expect new buyout activity and an unlocking of distributions. That said, liquidity and capital solutions providers will likely remain important to work through the substantial backlog of legacy investments. As a result, we continue to see compelling opportunities across secondaries, midlife co-investments and capital solutions. We are cautious on core private real estate, but this is offset by what we see as abundant market-dislocation opportunities in the value-add and opportunistic sectors and, particularly, real estate secondaries. Neuberger’s deep relationships and unique position within the private equity ecosystem have translated into record levels of deal flow across our platform.

data.title: Brookfield Infrastructure Partners: Buy This Solid Yield While It's Undervalued
data.description:
The AI revolution may be in full swing, but investing in AI names like Oracle (ORCL) may be a risky proposition full of ups and downs. This is driven by lofty expectations amid high valuations and anticipation of high capital commitments as it relates to spending on chips and infrastructure. That’s why I’m happy to take a more pragmatic investment approach, opting for infrastructure stocks that generate steady and recurring income.

This brings me to Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP), which I last covered in August, highlighting its inflation-protected, contractually-backed cash flows, growth catalysts, and attractive valuation. BIP has since gone on to produce a 21% total return, including distributions since my last piece, far outpacing the 6.8% rise in the S&P 500 (SPY) over the same timeframe.

As shown below, BIP still has plenty of ground to make up, as its total return has trailed that of the tech-heavy SPY over the past 5 years, despite having significant infrastructure that supports the tech sector.

In this article, I revisit BIP, including recent business results, and discuss what makes it a solid ‘Buy’ at present for income and potentially strong total returns, so let’s get started!

Why BIP?

Brookfield Infrastructure Partners issues a Schedule K-1 and is a globally diversified owner and operator of critical infrastructure assets. Its assets include those found in utilities, transport, midstream energy, and data businesses, which deliver essential services that are the backbone of modern economies. Since 2009, BIP has delivered an FFO/unit CAGR of 14% (10% CAGR over the past 5 years).

Recently, BIP has been growing its footprint in data infrastructure, including telecom towers, fiber networks, and hyperscale data centers. Its business model emphasizes long-life assets with high barriers to entry and contracted or regulated cash flows, which represent 85% of FFO. As shown below, BIP’s assets are diversified and span across five continents.

BIP continues to deliver strong results, with Q3 2025 FFO of $654 million, rising by 9% YoY on a per-unit basis to $0.83. Growth was driven by robust organic performance across the portfolio, even as there were timing differences between dispositions of assets sold in prior periods and partial quarter contributions from new assets.

Notably, the Utilities segment produced $190 million in FFO, slightly ahead of the prior year, supported by inflation indexing and the addition of over $450 million in rate base capital. These gains were partially offset by higher borrowing costs and the sale of its Mexican regulated natural gas transmission business earlier this year.

The Transport segment’s FFO was lower due to capital recycling, including divestments in an Australian export terminal and container assets. However, like-for-like results were slightly ahead of the prior year, supported by strong freight volumes and rate increases on rail networks and toll roads. The Midstream segment delivered a 6% YoY increase in FFO, reflecting heightened customer activity and utilization, especially in the Canadian midstream operations.

Last but not least, Data emerged as the standout growth engine for BIP, with FFO growing by more than 60% YoY to $138 million. This was driven by full-quarter results from a cell tower portfolio acquisition in India from American Tower (AMT). Additionally, BIP saw organic data center growth and commissioning of new capacity across hyperscale facilities.

Management is guiding for 8-9% annual FFO growth in the medium term. This is supported by 3-4% growth from inflationary indexing, 1-2% from volume upside, and 2-3% from reinvestment of cash flows. Recent catalysts include the $1.3 billion purchase of New Zealand natural gas infrastructure and a $1 billion investment in a South Korean industrial gas business (supported by take-or-pay contracts), serving semiconductor manufacturers.

Management believes that BIP is now at an inflection point with strong growth catalysts stemming from the next wave of digitization and targets 12% to 15% total annual return on invested capital. Data infrastructure is the next pillar of growth, supported by heavy investments from big and small tech companies, including Oracle, Microsoft (MSFT), and Google (GOOG) (GOOGL).

BIP expects to deploy up to $500 million annually to support growth in the Data segment. As shown below, Hyperscaler capital expenditures are expected to grow by 50% this year, and Semiconductor chips supporting future AI workloads are expected to grow by 5-10x this year.

BIP maintains a solid BBB+ rated balance sheet with $5.5 billion of liquidity and a weighted average debt term of 8 years. Just 1% of its non-recourse debt is maturing over the next 12 months, and 90% of its debt is held at a fixed rate. Importantly, the distribution is well-covered by a 67% FFO payout ratio (based on Q3’25 results) and sits 6% higher compared to last year. Over the past 12 years, BIP has an 8% distribution CAGR.

Lastly, I find BIP to be reasonably attractive at the current price of $35.10 with a forward P/FFO ratio of 10.6. I believe this is good value considering that analysts estimate 10-11% annual FFO/unit growth over the next two years, which is more or less what management is guiding over this timeframe. This, combined with the 4.9% distribution yield, could result in total returns in the mid-teens.

Risks to BIP include regulatory and political risks across its global footprint. Shifts in rate-setting mechanisms, taxation, and environmental policies could change the investment returns calculus for BIP. Moreover, BIP is dependent on rational and liquid capital markets to support its strategy of buying and selling undervalued and overvalued assets, respectively. Cost inflation could impact project costs, and increased competition for AI infrastructure assets could reduce initial investment yields.

Investor Takeaway

Brookfield Infrastructure Partners still looks like a solid choice for investors who want steady income and sensible growth without chasing high-multiple AI names. Its contracted and inflation-linked cash flows, growing data footprint, and recent acquisitions support management’s outlook for high-single-digit FFO growth.

BIP offers a clearer path to mid-teens total returns than many flashier AI plays, with a solid growth runway, a nearly 5% yield, and a valuation that doesn’t overreach. Considering all the above, I maintain a ‘Strong Buy’ rating on BIP for potential double-digit total returns annually from here.

data.title: Brace For A Weak November Labor Market Report
data.description:
The November labor market report

The US Bureau of Labor Statistics is set to release the labor market report for October and November on December 16th. The data is likely to be very noisy due to the government shutdown that extended from October to early November, during which the BLS did not even collect the data.

Given the alternative labor market data released during this period, we know that the labor market is significantly slowing. However, we don't know how many jobs have been permanently lost in the government sector, and we could get some insights about this on December 16th.

Thus, the November labor market report is likely to be very weak, confirming the loss of private jobs, in addition to possibly a large loss of government jobs.

However, we will not be able to determine the extent of the permanent layoffs in the government sector, for this, we will need to wait for the December labor market report.

Even after the data normalizes in December, the Fed Chair Powell warned that the payrolls report is likely overestimating the job creation by possibly 60K and that the labor market is possibly weaker than what the data suggests.

The official labor market assessment

The market consensus is that the labor market is currently in a no-fire/no-hire state.

This means that employers are currently not hiring, and this is likely for several reasons: 1) there is some uncertainty due to tariffs and broader economic worries, 2) lower labor supply due to the immigration policy, which is at the same time lowering the demand for labor, and 3) the AI adoption in some sectors.

However, the consensus is that at the same time, employers are not laying off the existing workers as consumption remains solid and there is a lack of supply of qualified workers in some industries, as well as a labor shortage in other industries.

Further, the official assessment is that the current labor market conditions are likely to persist, with the unemployment rate remaining stable at 4.4%, which is very close to full employment.

This is the Fed's assessment of labor market conditions, shared by most of the Wall Street analysts.

The soft data disagrees and paints a grim picture

The soft data does not support the official assessment of the labor market. Broadly, the soft data shows widespread layoffs, particularly in small companies and cyclical sectors—and this is consistent with a recession.

The ADP labor market data shows that there were -32K jobs lost in November and that there were job losses in 4 out of the last 6 months. The job losses are occurring in small companies, which are affected by the current economic conditions the most. In addition, there were significant job losses in cyclical industries, such as construction, manufacturing, information, professional & business services, and even finance.

Challenger Jobs Cuts report shows that "through November, employers have announced 1,170,821 job cuts, the most since 2020", and that hiring is the slowest in 15 years.

ISM Services Employment index is at 48.9, meaning service industries are lowering headcount, and the number has been below 50 for 8 out of the last 9 months. The ISM Manufacturing Employment Index is well below 50 at 44, and it has been below 50 for the last 10 months, suggesting deep job cuts in the manufacturing sector.

In support, the recent hard data has also been very negative, with the unemployment rate gradually rising and the non-farm payrolls confirming the trends from the ADP report, with significant job losses in cyclical sectors, with job creation mostly in the Health sector and some in Leisure & Hospitality.

So, there is a clear disconnect between the private data (and recent official hard data) and the official assessment of the labor market conditions.

The only official hard data indicator that supports the "no fire" assessment is the initial claims for unemployment, which are still very low, sub 250K. So, that's the confusion. Why are initial claims so low when all other data points to a significantly weakening labor market condition?

Why so optimistic for 2026?

Therefore, what we have is a significantly weakening labor market, consistent with a recession, except it has not been confirmed by the initial claims. However, corporate earnings are rising, and consumer continues to spend, pushing the GDP higher. Thus, whatever weakness the labor market has been showing can be ignored—it has not been affecting the economy, at least not yet.

Consequently, the official consensus for 2026 is positive. The labor market is not expected to improve, but it will not continue to deteriorate either—that's the key assumption.

So, what will stop the current trend of deteriorating labor market (rising unemployment rate and job losses)?

First, the assumption is that the stock market will continue to rise and the housing market will not slow, which is expected to keep spending strong in 2026 due to the wealth effect.

Second, the assumption is that fiscal stimulus in 2026 will boost the consumption by the less affluent population, which is currently struggling, also helped by the recent interest rate cuts.

But most importantly, the assumption is that there will be a heavy DFI investment in 2026, which will boost construction and manufacturing jobs.

In my opinion, the positive outlook is based on weak assumptions.

First, the DFI investment could be delayed if the Supreme Court rules against the Trump tariffs. Note, manufacturing and construction sectors are currently losing jobs, so if there is any ongoing DFI, it's not affecting the job market as intended.

Second, the AI adoption will continue to cause layoffs in some industries, irrespective of fiscal/monetary stimulus. In addition, companies will continue to lay off workers due to tariffs to protect margins. The tariffs are likely to stay under the different statute, even if the current Tariffs are ruled illegal.

Third, the AI bubble could burst in 2026, irrespective of fiscal/monetary stimulus. In addition, the housing market could sharply weaken in 2026 due to lower demand caused by the restrictive immigration policy. Thus, the wealth effect could turn negative in 2026.

Thus, in my opinion, the labor market will continue to weaken in 2026, and the economy is likely to slip into a recession, which means the investors could be facing a recessionary bear market with a bubble burst. With a significant correction in the S&P 500 (SP500).

Key Implication

The November labor market report will likely be very weak, which could trigger a recessionary scare, causing the repricing of interest rates for deeper Fed cuts in 2026, debunking the Fed's official labor market assessment.
